# Test: F-string interpolation with class hierarchy and formatting
# Complex test combining f-strings with inheritance, virtual/override, and control flow

@abstract
class Report:
    title: str
    report_id: int

    def __init__(self, title: str, id: int):
        self.title = title
        self.report_id = id

    @abstract
    def get_summary(self) -> str:
        ...

    @virtual
    def get_header(self) -> str:
        return f"Report #{self.report_id}: {self.title}"

class SalesReport(Report):
    revenue: float
    units_sold: int

    def __init__(self, title: str, id: int, revenue: float, units: int):
        super().__init__(title, id)
        self.revenue = revenue
        self.units_sold = units

    @override
    def get_summary(self) -> str:
        avg_price: float = self.revenue / self.units_sold if self.units_sold > 0 else 0.0
        return f"Revenue: ${self.revenue:.2f}, Units: {self.units_sold}, Avg: ${avg_price:.2f}"

    @override
    def get_header(self) -> str:
        return f"[SALES] {super().get_header()}"

class InventoryReport(Report):
    items_in_stock: int
    items_needed: int

    def __init__(self, title: str, id: int, in_stock: int, needed: int):
        super().__init__(title, id)
        self.items_in_stock = in_stock
        self.items_needed = needed

    @override
    def get_summary(self) -> str:
        status: str = "OK" if self.items_in_stock >= self.items_needed else "LOW"
        return f"Stock: {self.items_in_stock}, Needed: {self.items_needed}, Status: {status}"

    @override
    def get_header(self) -> str:
        return f"[INVENTORY] {super().get_header()}"

def process_reports(reports: list[Report]) -> None:
    count: int = 0
    for report in reports:
        count += 1
        print(f"Processing report {count}...")
        print(report.get_header())
        print(report.get_summary())

def main():
    reports: list[Report] = []
    
    sales1: SalesReport = SalesReport("Q1 Electronics", 101, 45000.75, 150)
    sales2: SalesReport = SalesReport("Q2 Accessories", 102, 12500.00, 250)
    inventory1: InventoryReport = InventoryReport("Warehouse A", 201, 500, 450)
    inventory2: InventoryReport = InventoryReport("Warehouse B", 202, 100, 300)
    
    reports.append(sales1)
    reports.append(sales2)
    reports.append(inventory1)
    reports.append(inventory2)
    
    print(f"Total reports to process: {len(reports)}")
    process_reports(reports)
    print(f"Report processing complete!")

# EXPECTED OUTPUT:
# Total reports to process: 4
# Processing report 1...
# [SALES] Report #101: Q1 Electronics
# Revenue: $45000.75, Units: 150, Avg: $300.01
# Processing report 2...
# [SALES] Report #102: Q2 Accessories
# Revenue: $12500.00, Units: 250, Avg: $50.00
# Processing report 3...
# [INVENTORY] Report #201: Warehouse A
# Stock: 500, Needed: 450, Status: OK
# Processing report 4...
# [INVENTORY] Report #202: Warehouse B
# Stock: 100, Needed: 300, Status: LOW
# Report processing complete!
